learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is used to express past experiences with emphasis

examples.english: I went to a new restaurant yesterday (and experienced it).
That movie was really interesting (from my experience).

learning_materials.notes: Emphasizes personal experience in the past

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this connects past actions with their results

examples.english: When I opened the door, it was raining.
When I called, nobody answered.

learning_materials.notes: Shows cause and effect in past experiences

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is indirect question form for asking about living

examples.english: I asked where they live.
They were curious if I live alone.

learning_materials.notes: Indirect question form with 냐고

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher showed how to express suggestions in past tense with results

examples.korean: 영화 보러 가자고 했더니 좋다고 했어요.
examples.english: When I suggested going to see a movie, they said it was good.
When I suggested studying together, they said they were busy.

learning_materials.notes: Combines suggestion (자고) with past experience pattern

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this can be shortened from 질문했더니 to just 했더니

examples.english: When I asked the teacher a question, they kindly explained it.
When I asked for directions, they told me in detail.

learning_materials.notes: Can be simplified to just 했더니 in casual speech

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means to divide something and give it to others

examples.english: I shared the snacks with my friends.
The teacher distributed pencils to the students.

learning_materials.notes: Combines 나누다 (divide) + 주다 (give)

learning_materials.english: it was piled up (from my observation)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used this to describe snow being piled up

examples.korean: 책상 위에 책이 높게 쌓여 있더라고요.
examples.english: Books were piled high on the desk.
Laundry was piled up like a mountain.

learning_materials.notes: Uses passive form with past experience ending

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is used for 'I' feeling sleepy, different from 잠이 들다

examples.english: I'm getting sleepy from studying late at night.
I started getting sleepy in the warm room.

learning_materials.notes: Used for first person; 잠이 들다 is used for third person

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is used for other people falling asleep

examples.english: The baby fell asleep in mom's arms.
I fell asleep on the sofa because I was tired.

learning_materials.notes: Used for third person or completed action

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in example about calling someone who didn't come to school

examples.english: I caught a cold because the weather was cold.
I couldn't go to work because I caught a cold.

learning_materials.notes: Common health expression using 걸리다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Mentioned in reading about Korean culture and relationships

examples.english: Interpersonal relationships are important at work.
I try hard to build good relationships with people.

learning_materials.notes: Important concept in Korean culture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used to describe Steve's reaction to Korean eating culture

examples.english: I was flustered because I suddenly received a question.
I was very confused because I got lost.

learning_materials.notes: Common emotional reaction verb

learning_materials.english: family members (literally: people who eat together)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained the etymology - people who eat rice together

examples.english: Our family has five members in total.
All family members gather during holidays.

learning_materials.notes: More intimate than 가족, emphasizes eating together

learning_materials.english: elderly person (polite way to say old person)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher taught this as a polite alternative to 노인

examples.korean: 지하철에서 나이 드신 분께 자리를 양보했어요.
examples.english: I gave up my seat to an elderly person on the subway.

examples.korean: 나이 드신 분들이 공원에서 운동하고 계세요.
examples.english: Elderly people are exercising in the park.

learning_materials.notes: More respectful than 노인 (old person)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher suggested using this instead of 재미있다 for cultural topics

examples.english: Korean history is really fascinating.
The content of that book was very interesting.

learning_materials.notes: More formal/academic than 재미있다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in expression about Korean culture becoming more interesting

examples.english: The more I learn Korean, the more difficult it becomes.
The more I get to know that person, the more I like them.

learning_materials.notes: Pattern: verb + 면 + same verb + 수록

learning_materials.english: to prepare so much food that the table legs might break
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this traditional expression about preparing lots of food

examples.english: During holidays, we always prepare a huge feast.
We prepared an enormous amount of food because guests were coming.

learning_materials.notes: Traditional Korean expression, 상 refers to low traditional table

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as alternative to 힘든 점

examples.korean: 이 아파트의 단점은 교통이 불편한 거예요.
examples.english: The disadvantage of this apartment is the inconvenient transportation.
Everything has advantages and disadvantages.

learning_materials.notes: 단 means 'short', opposite of 장점

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as alternative to 좋은 점

examples.korean: 온라인 수업의 장점은 집에서 들을 수 있다는 거예요.
examples.english: The advantage of online classes is that you can take them at home.

examples.korean: 그 사람의 가장 큰 장점은 성실함이에요.
examples.english: That person's biggest strength is their sincerity.

learning_materials.notes: 장 means 'long', opposite of 단점

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is considered a lucky dream in Korean superstition

examples.english: I had a pig dream last night.

examples.korean: 돼지꿈을 꾸면 좋은 일이 생긴다고 해요.
examples.english: They say good things happen when you dream of pigs.

learning_materials.notes: Considered lucky in Korean superstition

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained magpies are considered good luck, especially on New Year's Day

examples.korean: 아침에 까치가 울면 좋은 일이 있다고 해요.
examples.english: They say good things happen when magpies cry in the morning.
A magpie is crying in front of the house.

learning_materials.notes: Symbol of good luck in Korean culture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained it's good for birthdays but bad luck before exams

examples.english: We eat seaweed soup on birthdays.
Mom made seaweed soup for me.

learning_materials.notes: Birthday tradition, but avoid before exams due to slippery texture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means both physical falling and failing exams

examples.english: I'm upset because I failed the exam.
Leaves fell to the ground.

learning_materials.notes: Used for both physical falling and failing tests

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected student's pronunciation and explained difference from 비슷하다

examples.english: They have exactly the same face because they're twins.
Yesterday's and today's weather are exactly the same.

learning_materials.notes: Stronger than 비슷하다 (similar)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained people buy lottery tickets after dreaming of poop

examples.english: I bought a lottery ticket and won first prize.

examples.korean: 매주 복권을 사지만 당첨된 적이 없어요.
examples.english: I buy lottery tickets every week but have never won.

learning_materials.notes: 복 means luck, 권 means ticket

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher mentioned superstition about not walking under ladders

examples.english: You need a ladder to climb to high places.
Please climb the ladder carefully.

learning_materials.notes: Walking under ladders considered bad luck

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means having bad luck

examples.korean: 오늘 재수가 없어서 계속 안 좋은 일만 생겨요.
examples.english: I have bad luck today, so only bad things keep happening.
I had bad luck on the exam day.

learning_materials.notes: 재수 refers to luck or fortune

learning_materials.english: There's nothing special prepared, but please eat a lot
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this as a humble way hosts speak to guests

examples.english: There's nothing special prepared, but please eat a lot.
It's nothing special, but please eat a lot.

learning_materials.notes: Humble expression used by hosts

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher distinguished this from 상 (traditional low table)

examples.english: I eat dinner with my family at the dining table.
I bought a new dining table.

learning_materials.notes: Modern dining table, different from traditional 상

learning_materials.english: to eat noodles (meaning: to get married)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this traditional expression means getting married

examples.english: When are you going to let us eat noodles? (When will you get married?)
The day to eat noodles has finally come. (The wedding day has come.)

learning_materials.notes: Traditional expression because noodles were served at weddings

learning_materials.english: What a stroke of luck! (unexpected good fortune)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this expresses surprise at unexpected good fortune

examples.english: 50% discount? What a stroke of luck!

examples.korean: 갑자기 보너스를 받다니, 이게 웬 떡이야!
examples.english: Getting a bonus suddenly? What unexpected good fortune!

learning_materials.notes: Used when something unexpectedly good happens

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means paying too much money, like wearing a gourd over your eyes

examples.english: I got ripped off at the tourist spot.
Don't get overcharged at that store.

learning_materials.notes: 바가지 is a gourd bowl, metaphor for being blind to fair prices

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means socializing over drinks, not just one drink

examples.korean: 퇴근 후에 동료들과 한 잔 했어요.
examples.english: I had drinks with colleagues after work.

examples.korean: 오랜만에 친구와 한 잔 하자고 했어요.
examples.english: I suggested having drinks with a friend after a long time.

learning_materials.notes: Implies social drinking time, not literally one drink

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means when someone doesn't show up for a meeting

examples.english: My friend didn't come, so I got stood up.
I've been stood up on a date before.

learning_materials.notes: Literally means 'to meet the wind' - waiting alone

learning_materials.english: to know many people/have wide connections
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means having many acquaintances

examples.korean: 그 사람은 발이 넓어서 모르는 사람이 없어요.
examples.english: That person has wide connections, so there's no one they don't know.
Having wide connections makes work easier.

learning_materials.notes: Metaphor: wide feet can go many places = know many people

learning_materials.english: to fall in love at first sight
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means falling in love when first seeing someone

examples.english: I fell in love with my husband at first sight and married him.
I fell in love with that actor at first sight.

learning_materials.notes: 첫눈 = first sight, 반하다 = to fall for someone

learning_materials.english: as difficult as picking stars from the sky (extremely difficult)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this means something extremely difficult or impossible

examples.korean: 그 대학에 들어가는 건 하늘의 별 따기예요.
examples.english: Getting into that university is like picking stars from the sky.

examples.korean: 완벽한 사람을 찾는 건 하늘의 별 따기죠.
examples.english: Finding a perfect person is like picking stars from the sky.

learning_materials.notes: Expresses impossibility or extreme difficulty

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is used to confirm information you heard from someone

examples.english: I heard it's going to rain tomorrow?
I heard you moved here recently.

learning_materials.notes: Used to confirm information, similar to 'I heard that...'

learning_materials.english: I heard that... (for nouns with 이다)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this is used with nouns ending in 이다

examples.english: I heard you're a doctor?
I heard you're Korean.

learning_materials.notes: Used with 이다 verbs, 이 can be omitted

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this expresses worry about future possibilities

examples.english: I went quickly because I was worried about being late.
I brought an umbrella in case it rains.

learning_materials.notes: Expresses concern about future events

learning_materials.english: I'm worried I might make a mistake
learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in dialogue about job interview anxiety

examples.english: I'm worried I might make a mistake during the presentation.
I'm careful when driving because I'm worried about making mistakes.

learning_materials.notes: Common expression for performance anxiety

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of worrying about exam results

examples.english: I studied all night because I was worried about failing the exam.
I'm nervous because I'm worried about failing the interview.

learning_materials.notes: Common worry expression for tests and applications

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used this as example of worry expression

examples.english: I started exercising because I was worried about gaining weight.
I don't eat late-night snacks because I'm worried about gaining weight.

learning_materials.notes: Common worry, especially among young people

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of worrying about graduate school decision

examples.english: I decided carefully because I was worried about regretting it later.
I applied because I was worried about regretting missing the opportunity.

learning_materials.notes: Used for major life decisions

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained three different forms of expressing worry

examples.english: I'm worried about the exam.
I'm worried about my friend.

learning_materials.notes: Three different grammatical patterns for same meaning

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used to describe job market competition

examples.english: Job competition is fierce these days.

examples.korean: 그 학교는 입학 경쟁이 심하다고 들었어요.
examples.english: I heard the admission competition for that school is fierce.

learning_materials.notes: Common expression for competitive situations

learning_materials.context_from_class: Mentioned in context of job interviews and career fit

examples.korean: 이 일이 제 적성에 맞는 것 같아요.
examples.english: This job seems to suit my aptitude.
I took an aptitude test.

learning_materials.notes: Important for career discussions

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in job interview context

examples.english: I passed the written exam and got to have an interview.
The written exam was really difficult.

learning_materials.notes: Common in job application processes

learning_materials.context_from_class: Main topic of the dialogue about job applications

examples.english: I have a company interview tomorrow.
I want to make a good impression in the interview.

learning_materials.notes: Essential vocabulary for job seekers

learning_materials.context_from_class: Mentioned as workplace in job interview context

examples.english: Working at a broadcasting station is my dream.
That broadcasting station is very popular.

learning_materials.notes: Specific workplace mentioned in dialogue

learning_materials.context_from_class: Job position mentioned in interview context

examples.english: I work as a newspaper reporter.

examples.korean: 기자가 되려면 글을 잘 써야 해요.
examples.english: To become a journalist, you need to write well.

learning_materials.notes: Professional occupation

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of job hunting difficulty

examples.english: I got a job after graduating from university.
I want to get a job at a good company.

learning_materials.notes: Important verb for career discussions

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of job applications

examples.english: I applied to that company.
I plan to apply to several places.

learning_materials.notes: Essential for application processes

learning_materials.context_from_class: Opposite of 떨어지다, mentioned in exam context

examples.english: I'm happy because I passed the exam.
I finally got accepted to university.

learning_materials.notes: Opposite of 떨어지다 (to fail)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Alternative way to express failing, mentioned by teacher

examples.english: Unfortunately, I failed the interview.

examples.korean: 첫 번째 시험에서 불합격했지만 포기하지 않았어요.
examples.english: I failed the first exam but didn't give up.

learning_materials.notes: More formal than 떨어지다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected student's usage, explaining proper form

examples.english: I can't fall asleep because I have many worries.

examples.korean: 커피를 너무 많이 마셔서 잠이 안 와요.
examples.english: I can't fall asleep because I drank too much coffee.

correction_details.explanation: Sleep 'comes' to you, you don't 'do' sleep

learning_materials.notes: 잠 comes to you, different from English thinking

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher suggested this as better alternative to student's sentence

examples.english: I woke up in the morning and opened the window.
I opened the window because it was stuffy.

correction_details.explanation: Need specific action that leads to discovering snow

learning_materials.notes: More logical sequence for discovering weather changes

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher suggested as alternative action for discovering snow

examples.english: I looked outside because there was a noise.
I looked outside because I was curious about the weather.

learning_materials.notes: Natural action before discovering weather conditions

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in example about speaking informally to neighbors

examples.english: I speak informally to close friends.

examples.korean: 처음 만난 사람에게 반말을 하면 안 돼요.
examples.english: You shouldn't speak informally to someone you just met.

learning_materials.notes: Important concept in Korean social hierarchy

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used as result of speaking informally to neighbors

examples.korean: 아이가 말을 안 들어서 화를 냈어요.
examples.english: I got angry because the child didn't listen.

examples.korean: 화를 내지 말고 차근차근 설명해 주세요.
examples.english: Don't get angry, please explain step by step.

learning_materials.notes: Common emotional expression

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in example about buying a car after getting paid

examples.english: I receive my salary on the 25th of every month.
When I receive my salary, I give my parents spending money.

learning_materials.notes: Essential workplace vocabulary

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in example about spending salary money

examples.english: I finally bought a new car.
I'm saving money to buy a car.

learning_materials.notes: Major purchase decision

learning_materials.context_from_class: Result of buying expensive car with salary

examples.korean: 쇼핑을 너무 많이 해서 돈이 다 없어졌어요.
examples.english: I shopped too much, so all my money is gone.
All my money is gone from traveling.

learning_materials.notes: Common result of overspending

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in exercise example causing tiredness

examples.english: I played tennis with friends on the weekend.
Playing tennis is good exercise.

learning_materials.notes: Popular recreational sport

learning_materials.context_from_class: Result of playing tennis or not sleeping well

examples.english: I went to bed late yesterday, so I'm tired today.
I'm tired because I worked too much.

learning_materials.notes: Very common daily adjective

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained this can be shortened to just 배고프다고 했더니

examples.english: The child said they were hungry, so I gave them food.
When I said I was hungry, they made food for me.

learning_materials.notes: 말하다 can be omitted in casual speech

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of response to saying you're hungry

examples.english: The teacher shared bread with the students.
My friend shared delicious bread with me.

learning_materials.notes: Act of kindness and sharing

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected student's expression about snow

examples.english: Snow piled up a lot overnight.
Snow is piled high in the yard.

learning_materials.notes: Proper way to describe snow accumulation

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher contrasted this with snow, which makes no sound

examples.english: I can hear the sound of rain.
The sound of rain on the window is loud.

learning_materials.notes: Rain makes sound, but snow doesn't

learning_materials.english: you can't hear the sound of snow falling
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained snow falls silently unlike rain

examples.english: Even when it snows, there's no sound.
Snow falls quietly.

learning_materials.notes: Important distinction from rain
